Applying A Reusable Plot Theme

Goal

Reusable code is useful when you would otherwise copy and paste the same styling across many figures.

This notebook shows the basic reusable-theme move:

  1. Make a basic graph.
  2. Define one theme function.
  3. Apply the function: + theme_name()

Load Data

states <- readr::read_csv("data/real/state_population_ranks.csv")
Rows: 51 Columns: 4
── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: ","
chr (2): state, state_name
dbl (2): population, rank

ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
countries <- readr::read_csv("data/real/infant_mortality_countries.csv")
Rows: 105 Columns: 6
── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: ","
chr (3): country_name, region, oil_exporting_country
dbl (2): income_usd_per_capita, infant_mortality_per_1000
lgl (1): oil_exporter

ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.

Prepare Data

Data preparation step Functions in this chunk
Keep or remove rows filter()
Create or change columns mutate() 
top_states <- states |>
  filter(rank <= 15) |>
  mutate(state_name = reorder(state_name, population))

infant_mortality <- countries |>
  filter(!is.na(infant_mortality_per_1000))

Basic Graph

Start with the plot. Do not worry about styling yet.

Plot component Code in this chunk
Aesthetic mappings x; y
Geoms / stats geom_point()
geom_point() properties alpha; size
Scales / coordinates / facets / labels scale_x_log10(); labs() 
p1 <- ggplot(
  infant_mortality,
  aes(x = income_usd_per_capita, y = infant_mortality_per_1000)
) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.75, size = 2.5) +
  scale_x_log10(
    breaks = c(70, 100, 300, 1000, 3000, 6000),
    labels = c("$70", "$100", "$300", "$1,000", "$3,000", "$6,000")
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Income And Infant Mortality Across Countries",
    x = "Income per person",
    y = "Infant mortality per 1,000 live births"
  )

p1

One Reusable Theme

Now put the repeated styling into one function.

Plot component Code in this chunk
Scales / coordinates / facets / labels theme_minimal(); theme() 
theme_jhu <- function() {
  update_geom_defaults("bar", list(fill = "#002D72"))
  update_geom_defaults("point", list(color = "#002D72"))

  theme_minimal(base_family = "Tahoma") +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_text(
        family = "Georgia",
        size = 18,
        hjust = 0.5,
        face = "bold",
        margin = margin(b = 10)
      ),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(
        hjust = 0.5,
        family = "Tahoma",
        size = 12,
        margin = margin(t = 5, b = 15)
      ),
      plot.caption = element_text(
        family = "Tahoma",
        size = 10,
        color = "#7E7E7C",
        margin = margin(t = 15)
      ),
      axis.text.y = element_text(
        family = "Tahoma",
        size = 9,
        color = "#2C2C33"
      ),
      axis.text.x = element_text(
        family = "Tahoma",
        size = 9,
        color = "#2C2C33"
      ),
      axis.title.y = element_text(
        family = "Tahoma",
        size = 11,
        margin = margin(r = 15),
        face = "bold"
      ),
      axis.title.x = element_text(
        family = "Tahoma",
        size = 11,
        margin = margin(t = 15),
        face = "bold"
      ),
      axis.line.x = element_blank(),
      axis.line.y = element_blank(),
      panel.grid.major.x = element_line(
        color = "#E5E2E0",
        linewidth = 0.5,
        linetype = "solid"
      ),
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
    )
}

It’s often useful to save this function in a file you load at the top of every script/notebook or in a bootstrap() function.

Apply The Theme

Add the theme to the plot.

Plot component Code in this chunk
Scales / coordinates / facets / labels theme_jhu() 
p1 +
  theme_jhu()

Now you have a fully styled plot without an overwhelming amount of code.

Apply The Same Theme To Another Plot

The value comes in consistent styling without repeating yourself. You can still override pieces for a specific plot.

Plot component Code in this chunk
Aesthetic mappings x; y
Geoms / stats geom_bar()
Scales / coordinates / facets / labels coord_flip(); scale_y_continuous(); labs(); theme_jhu(); theme() 
p2 <- ggplot(top_states, aes(x = state_name, y = population)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity") +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::label_number(scale_cut = scales::cut_short_scale())) +
  labs(
    title = "Largest States By Population",
    x = "State name",
    y = "Population"
  )

p2 +
  theme_jhu() +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank()
  )

The last theme() call removes the population-axis gridlines for this plot only. It does not change theme_jhu().

When to Extract

A good rule of thumb: extract a function the third time you repeat the same code.

Takeaways

  • A theme is just a function that includes a lot of styling boilerplate.
  • Add it to a plot like you would any other ggplot2 feature. Here, we use + theme_jhu().
  • Keep data choices in the plot code, not the theme function.
